coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-embedded amorphous nickel oxide for highly enhanced photocatalytic redox performance
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Cai, Tingting
Chang, Qing
Xue, Chaorui
Li, Ning
Yang, Jinlong
Hu, Shengliang
coredata.dc:description: Amorphous materials reveal promising prospects in photocatalysis for the abundant active sites and tunable electronic configuration due to the lattice flexibility. However, the intrinsic lattice distortion could also cause the self-trapping effect and results in the recombination of photogenerated carriers. This disadvantage could be modified by the small size of amorphous domains for reducing charge migration distance. For this purpose, carbon dots (CDs) are introduced as the heterogeneous nucleus to regulate the configuration and composition of the amorphous nickel oxides for efficient utilization of photocarriers in catalytic reactions. The resultant nanocomposites a-NiOx/CDs show the densely distributed CDs embedded in the amorphous nickel oxide with dual valences of Ni2+ and Ni3+, and they reveal superior activities in photocatalytic oxidation and reduction compared with the single amorphous nickel oxide. The extraordinary photocatalytic performance is attributed to the synergetic function of their excellent separation efficiency of photoinduced charges and the cyclic conversion between Ni2+ and Ni3+ in the defects. This work provides inspiration for the modification and photocatalytic application of the amorphous materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Photocatalysis
Amorphous nickel oxide
Nanocomposite

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The schematic diagram of the a-NiOx/CDs synthesis process. (b) Zeta potential of CDs and CDs + Ni2+. (c) XRD patterns of a-NiOx, a-NiOx/CDs, and the standard PDF cards of NiO and Ni2O3. (d) The XPS Ni 2p3/2 peaks of a-NiOx/CDs and a-NiOx.

(a and b) TEM images of a-NiOx with different magnification. (c) HRTEM of a-NiOx. (d and e) TEM images of a-NiOx/CDs with different magnification. (f) HRTEM of a-NiOx/CDs. (g) Element mapping images of a-NiOx/CDs.

The catalytic performance of (a) a-NiOx/CDs and (b) a-NiOx with the illumination of visible light. (c) Comparison of the photocatalytic activity of a-NiOx/CDs and a-NiOx with and without light. (d) Comparison of the enzyme-like kinetics activity of a-NiOx/CDs and a-NiOx.

(a) The photocatalytic effect of a-NiOx/CDs to p-NP with light. (b) The photocatalytic effect of a-NiOx to p-NP with light. (c) Plots of ln (Ct/C0) to time for p-NP reduction by a-NiOx/CDs and a-NiOx with and without light. (d) The recycling performance of a-NiOx/CDs.

(a) The transient photocurrent responses and (b) electrochemical impedance spectra of a-NiOx/CDs and a-NiOx, (c) CV curves of a-NiOx/CDs and a-NiOx in 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution. (d) CV curves of a-NiOx/CDs and a-NiOx in Na2SO4 electrolyte with NaBH4 and p-NP.

Sacrificial agent experiment of a-NiOx/CDs in (a) TMB photocatalytic oxidation reaction and (b) p-NP photocatalytic reduction reaction. (c) The H2O2 generation performance of a-NiOx/CDs in pH 4. (d) Band structures of a-NiOx/CDs and a-NiOx. (e) The schematic illustration of the catalytic mechanism.
The bacteriostatic test of a-NiOx/CDs and a-NiOx to staphylococcus aureus in (a) dark and (b) light conditions.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-embedded amorphous nickel oxide for highly enhanced photocatalytic redox performance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Group of New Energy Materials and Devices, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Group of New Energy Materials and Devices

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Group of New Energy Materials and Devices, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lvliang University, Lvliang 033000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lvliang University, Lvliang 033000, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots embedded into amorphous nickel oxides overcome the internal consumption of photocarriers and effectively improve their transfer efficiency, resulting in the boosted photocatalytic activity in the redox reactions and antibacterial activity under visible light.

Amorphous materials reveal promising prospects in photocatalysis for the abundant active sites and tunable electronic configuration due to the lattice flexibility. However, the intrinsic lattice distortion could also cause the self-trapping effect and results in the recombination of photogenerated carriers. This disadvantage could be modified by the small size of amorphous domains for reducing charge migration distance. For this purpose, carbon dots (CDs) are introduced as the heterogeneous nucleus to regulate the configuration and composition of the amorphous nickel oxides for efficient utilization of photocarriers in catalytic reactions. The resultant nanocomposites a-NiOx/CDs show the densely distributed CDs embedded in the amorphous nickel oxide with dual valences of Ni2+ and Ni3+, and they reveal superior activities in photocatalytic oxidation and reduction compared with the single amorphous nickel oxide. The extraordinary photocatalytic performance is attributed to the synergetic function of their excellent separation efficiency of photoinduced charges and the cyclic conversion between Ni2+ and Ni3+ in the defects. This work provides inspiration for the modification and photocatalytic application of the amorphous materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: High-efficient photocatalysts with tunable bandgap and broad applicability are of great significance for the conversion of solar energy to chemical products and storable energy, owing to their enormous potential for green chemistry and energy sustainability [1]. Cost-efficient transition metal oxides as semiconductors have been applied in pollutants degradation, H2 evolution, H2O2 production, and virus disinfection [2–6] for their good reactivity and changeable valence inherited from the unfulfilled d orbitals of the transition metal. Among them, nickel oxides (NiOx) with relatively high redox potentials and catalytic activity have attracted a growing interest in the field of photocatalysis, energy storage, sensors, magnetic resonance imaging, as well as nano-carriers for transferring drugs [7–9]. However, their limited visible light absorption efficiency and photoinduced charge generation ability restrict the photocatalytic activities [10].

Morphology and structure modification is an effective method to regulate the characteristics of nickel oxides. The plate-like NiO nanocrystals [8], honeycomb-like H-NiOx frameworks [11], and amorphous NiO [12,13] have been reported. Among them, amorphous structure has abundant defects and multiple valences owing to its distorted atomic arrangement of long-range disorder and short-range order. This intrinsic feature provides sufficient active sites and tunable electronic configurations for the photocatalytic redox [14]. However, the intrinsic lattice distortion would cause the self-trapping effect and result in rapid recombination of photoinduced charge carriers [13]. As a consequence, it has been a significant challenge to overcome this Achilles’ heel upon photocatalysis [12]. We conceive to minish the size and thickness of the amorphous domains by introducing proper nanocrystals into the system, thus reducing the transfer distance and improving the separation efficiency of the photoinduced charges.

As a rising star in carbon materials, carbon dots (CDs) have shown distinguished performance in photoelectrochemical and photocatalytic applications owing to their ease of surface functionalization, tunable light-harvesting range, sensitive photo-response, and outstanding electron-transfer efficiency [15–17]. Their excellent binding capacity with metal ions was indicated by the fluorescence quenching phenomenon of CDs as the detection probe of metal ions [18,19]. More recently, CDs were reported to prolong the lifetime of photocarriers as a selective hole acceptor for the abundant defects and more negative conduction levels, favouring the multi-electron reduction process [20,21].

Based on the above analysis, we propose a facile strategy to embed CDs into amorphous nickel oxides through the heterogeneous nucleation on CDs during the synthesis process. In the synthesized nanocomposites (a-NiOx/CDs), CDs densely embedded in the plum pudding model-like amorphous nickel oxide. Interestingly, the composites can photocatalyze both the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) and the reduction of p-nitrophenol (p-NP). Moreover, the superior photocatalytic activities of a-NiOx/CDs than the single amorphous nickel oxide demonstrate the conspicuous effects of the introduced CDs. We found that CDs serve as hole acceptors and facilitate the shift of photoinduced electrons to the active sites on the surface, thus activating the cycling conversion between Ni2+ and Ni3+ for the redox reaction as well as the antibacterial effect.

Firstly, CDs were fabricated from anthracite in the H2O2 solution according to the previous report of our group [22]. Then 0.4 mmol anhydrous NiCl2 was put into the 30 mL fresh CDs colloidal solution. After magnetic stirring at room temperature for 2 h, 0.1 M NaOH solution was added dropwise to adjust the pH (from 1.6 to 5.6). The suspension began to arise at about pH 3.0, and this phenomenon continues until pH 5.6. After stirring for 30 min continuously, the precipitate was separated in a centrifuge at a speed of 10,000 rpm, then cleaned with deionized water three times before vacuum drying at 60 °C. The prepared sample was then ground and preserved. The a-NiOx sample was prepared by the same procedures except that the CDs colloidal solution was replaced by the H2O2 solution.

TMB catalysis: 200 μL 30 mM TMB solution was added to 10 mL 0.1 M CH3COONa-CH3COOH buffer solution with pH 4.0, then 1 mg of the prepared sample was put into the solution. The mixture was stirred and irradiated under a xenon lamp with an optical filter of cut-420 nm to remove the UV irradiation, and the light intensity was set as 100 mW/cm2. The absorbance values at 652 nm were measured at set intervals by the UV–vis absorption spectrophotometer.

p-NP catalyst: The p-NP solution was prepared by mixing 3 mg p-NP and 50 mg NaBH4 into 200 mL of deionized water, which show a bright yellow color. Then 20 mL of the above solution was taken and put into 3 mg catalyst. The light intensity was also adjusted to 100 mW/cm2. In the process of p-NP reduction, it can be observed that the color gradually lightens until it becomes colorless. The concentration of p-NP was detected by UV–vis adsorption spectrophotometer since the characteristic absorption wavelength of p-NP is located at ∼398 nm.

Staphylococcus aureus was chosen as the research object and spread plate method was employed. Firstly, The bacterial strains were cultured on the Columbia blood agar medium at temperature of 37 ℃ for 24 h. Then the bacteria are diluted to 0.3 Tu of the turbidity, and the samples of a-NiOx/CDs and a-NiOx were mixed with the solution of bacteria at room temperature for 0.5 h. In the contrast experiment with light, the same systems were shined by a Xenon lamp source with a light intensity of 50 mW/cm2 for 10 min. After oscillation, the bacterial solution was diluted 100 times, and 50 μL was taken and cultured on the medium at 37 ℃ for 24 h.
The other experimental details and sample characterization methods were provided in supplementary information.

The preparation of a-NiOx/CDs was carried out in the fresh colloidal solution of CDs with the residual H2O2 (Fig. 1 a). The Ni2+ ions tend to be attracted to adsorb on the surface of CDs due to the opposite electronegativity of CDs and Ni2+, resulting in the decrease of the zeta potential illustrated in Fig. 1b. When NaOH was added, the nickel compound began to generate with CDs as the nucleation center. Finally, the nanocomposites were synthesized with the cooperative effect of CDs and H2O2. For comparison, the single a-NiOx was also synthesized in the solution without CDs. It is clear that both samples show different colors (Fig. S1).

Fig. 1c exhibits the highly similar XRD patterns of a-NiOx/CDs and a-NiOx with diffused peaks, which are the typical characteristics of the amorphous structure with the short-range order atomic arrangements and abundant defects. And it is reported that amorphous structure is more likely to form in an aqueous reaction at room temperature [14,23]. The two peaks at around 2θ = 38° and 60° correspond to the crystal planes (111) and (220) of NiO (JCPSD No.71-1179). Despite Ni(OH)2 also has a peak at 38.6° (Fig. S2), its strongest characteristic peak at 19.2° doesn’t accord with the results of a-NiOx/CDs and a-NiOx. Moreover, the feature peaks of Ni2O3 (JCPSD No.014-0481) at 31.9, 39.1, and 56.8° show intimate relevance to the main peaks in a-NiOx/CDs. In addition, the peaks at around 30° could be partly composed of CDs (Fig. S3). According to the analysis above, the reactions during the synthesis process are inferred as Eqs. (1) and (2).
(1)
NiCl 2
+ 2NaOH
→
Ni(OH) 2
+ 2NaCl
(2)
3 N
i(OH) 2
+ H
2
O 2
→
NiO + Ni
2
O 3
+ 4H
2
O

The elemental composition and surface chemical states of a-NiOx/CDs and a-NiOx were further identified by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The survey spectra (Fig. S4a) confirm the presence of C, O, and Ni in a-NiOx and a-NiOx/CDs. Wherein Ni 2p consists of Ni 2p1/2 and Ni 2p3/2 distributed at the bonding energy of ∼874, ∼856 eV and two satellite peaks at ∼881, ∼862 eV, respectively [24] (Fig. S4b). The high-resolution XPS spectra of Ni 2p3/2 peak in a-NiOx/CDs and a-NiOx reveal the coexistence of Ni2+ and Ni3+ at the bonding energy of 855.6 eV [25] and 856.7 eV [26,27] (Fig. 1d). The amount ratio Ni3+/Ni2+ is relatively 0.22 and 0.78 for a-NiOx and a-NiOx/CDs, respectively, confirming the regulation effect of CDs on the synthesis of the composite [28,29]. In addition, Fig. S4c and d demonstrate the CC, CO, and CO bonds in C 1s peak and NiO, CO, and CO peaks in O 1s, indicating the presence of both nickel oxides and CDs.

The detailed microstructures of a-NiOx/CDs and a-NiOx were explored by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The a-NiOx (Fig. 2 a and b) clearly reveal the atomic arrangements characteristic of long-range disorder and short-range order. This feature verifies the amorphous state in the XRD results. The high-resolution images (Fig. 2c) indicate the presence of NiO with the inter-planar spacing of 0.208 and 0.241 nm, according with the (111) and (200) lattice plane, respectively. In a-NiOx/CDs (Fig. 2d–f), it is observed that the embedded CDs are densely distributed in the amorphous substrate with inter-planar spacing of 0.21 nm. The whole structure is similar to the plum pudding model and the amorphous domains are spaced apart by CDs. Besides, plenty of thin NiO lattice layers around CDs can be clearly observed, reflecting the effective separation of the amorphous domains. The element mapping images further demonstrate the existence of the elements Ni, O, and C in a-NiOx/CDs (Fig. 2g).

As a commonly-used reaction substrate, TMB is commonly employed to evaluate the catalytic kinetics of the nano-enzyme and photocatalyst [30–32]. The two samples both exhibit enzyme-like property that TMB is oxidized to the blue product with the characteristic absorption peak at 652 nm under visible light irradiation (Fig. 3 a, b). The much higher peak intensity of a-NiOx/CDs indicates its better catalytic activity than a-NiOx. Without light irradiation, the photocatalytic activity of a-NiOx/CDs declines significantly, while a-NiOx remains a similar performance (Fig. 3c, S5). It could be speculated that the photo-response activity of a-NiOx/CDs is closely associated with the effective separation of the photoinduced charges. The performance of CDs to TMB was also provided for comparison, revealing a very limited activity (Fig. S6). The conditions of nickel sources (Fig. S7) and addition amount (Fig. S8) were also investigated. It was obtained that the sample prepared with 0.4 mmol NiCl2 exhibited the best performance. Furthermore, quantitative analysis of a-NiOx/CDs and a-NiOx are studied via enzyme kinetics based on the Michaelis-Menten equation. The maximum photocatalytic reaction rate (V max) of a-NiOx/CDs is 1.54 μM∙min−1, 2.6 times higher than the 0.59 μM∙min−1 of a-NiOx. Meanwhile, the Michaelis constants (K m) of both samples are 0.17 and 0.32 respectively, indicating the better affinity of a-NiOx/CDs to TMB than a-NiOx. Both the two parameters indicate the much better photocatalytic activity of a-NiOx/CDs to TMB oxidation than a-NiOx.

The p-NP reduction has vital value both in the basic photocatalytic mechanism research and industrial production [33,34]. Generally, the reduction ability of NaBH4 is impaired seriously in the absence of catalyst [35–37]. We interestingly discovered that a-NiOx/CDs exhibit noticeable photocatalytic activity to the reduction of p-NP in the presence of NaBH4 (Fig. 4 a), which opens up the application of a-NiOx/CDs in photocatalytic reduction. The reduction product is proved to be p-AP (Fig. S9) and its formation involves the multi-electron hydrogenation process. By contrast, a-NiOx (Fig. 4b) shows a much slower photocatalytic reduction rate, and CDs does not show any effect to the reaction (Fig. S10). Furthermore, the photocatalytic performance of a-NiOx/CDs and a-NiOx with and without light are obtained (Fig. 4c). The relationship of ln(C/C 0) versus time exhibits a linear correlation that accords with the pseudo first-order reaction kinetics [35] and the reaction rate can be received from the slope value. The a-NiOx/CDs show significant distinction in the presence and absence of light. While for a-NiOx, only an inconspicuous advantage is observed under light. Considering the electronic configuration of Ni (d8) that favors the adsorption of p-NP [34,38], it is deduced that larger amount of Ni2+/Ni3+ and the efficiency conversion of the photoinduced electrons both contribute to the better photocatalytic reduction activity of a-NiOx/CDs. Fig. S11 demonstrates the progressively elevated photocatalytic rate with the increase of the illumination intensity, further supporting the vital function of light to a-NiOx/CDs. More amount of a-NiOx/CDs can also result in faster color fading of p-NP for more active sites that supplied (Fig. S12). The reductant NaBH4 could also affect the reduction rate in a low addition amount (Fig. S13). When compared with other photocatalysts, a-NiOx/CDs exhibits relatively competitive performance (Tables S1 and S2), especially considering its dual photocatalytic function.

The reusability of a-NiOx/CDs in photocatalytic reduction is studied (Fig. 4d). It shows that the absorbance value was almost unchanged with the efficiency of ∼97 % after 25 min during four consecutive cycles, indicating the good stability of a-NiOx/CDs in the catalytic reduction of p-NP. Moreover, the identical XRD patterns and morphology images of the reused sample also demonstrate its stability and reliability (Fig. S14, 15).

To clarify the effect of illumination to a-NiOx/CDs and a-NiOx, the photo-response characteristics were studied through the electrochemical approach. In transient photocurrent experiment (Fig. 5 a), sudden surges and sharp falls can be observed in the I-t curve with the alternate light and dark conditions. Amorphous a-NiOx shows a much lower current, while the photocurrent density of a-NiOx/CDs increases by three times, revealing the vital function of CDs in the effective separation of the photoinduced charges. In the electrochemical impedance spectrum (EIS) (Fig. 5b), a-NiOx/CDs shows a Nyquist radius of 220 Ω, nearly half the value of a-NiOx. The Nyquist radius involve with the resistance of the electrode/electrolyte interface, and the smaller radius means the lower resistance and faster speed of the charge transfer process.

The electrochemical experiment of cyclic voltammetry (CV) was employed to analyze the redox reaction in the view of electron transfer. Fig. 5c shows the CV curves of a-NiOx/CDs and a-NiOx in 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution. The oxidation peak emerges at about 0.9 V at positive scans, while the corresponding reduction peak appears at about 0.7 V. This pair of redox peaks refers to the conversion of Ni2+/Ni3+ [8,39], and the higher current density of a-NiOx/CDs demonstrates more electrons involved in the electrochemical reaction. In the electrolyte with NaBH4 and p-NP (Fig. 5d), new oxidation peaks are observed at about 0 V and −0.4 V, and they could be related to the reduction of p-NP. As reported, the anodic peaks at about 0 V and −0.4 V correspond to the active hydrogen species of H*ads (adsorbed hydrogen on the catalyst surface) and H*abs (absorbed hydrogen in the catalyst lattices). It reveals the adsorption of H* on the active sites and this procedure is the necessity of the reduction reaction of p-NP.

It is necessary to figure out the dominant active radicals of a-NiOx/CDs in the photocatalysis to TMB and p-NP, since they directly participate in the photocatalysis. The presence of ·O2 – and ·OH were certified with the nitro blue tetrazolium (NBT) (Fig. S16a) and terephthalic acid PL (TA-PL) probing test (Fig. S16b) [40–42]. EDTA-2Na, NaIO3, vitamin C (VC), and isopropanol are employed as sacrifice reagents of holes (h+), electrons (e-), superoxide radical (∙O2 –), and hydroxyl radical (∙OH). Fig. 6 a shows the vital effect of h+ and ∙O2 – in the TMB oxidation. The improved absorbance with NaIO3 could be ascribed to the quicker photocarriers separation with the sacrifice of electrons. In the photocatalytic reduction of p-NP (Fig. 6b), the addition of NaIO3 totally cut off the reaction, revealing the principal effect of e- in the p-NP reduction. In addition, as one of the reactive oxygen species, H2O2 generation capacity of a-NiOx/CDs was measured with potassium titanium oxalate reagent [43]. It was observed that H2O2 could be slowly generated under light (Fig. 6c), which is speculated to be based on the reaction between H2O and the photo-induced holes under light [40] (Eq. (3)). Consequently, ∙O2 – could generate via the conversion of H2O2 (Eq. (4)).
(3)
2H 2
O + 2h
+
→
H 2
O 2
+ 2
H
+
(4)
H 2
O 2
+ 2h
+
=
·
O
2
-
+ 2H
+

Because of the associated relationship between band structure and active radicals, the bandgap of a-NiOx/CDs and a-NiOx was studied and obtained through the XPS valence bands (VB) spectrum and the Mott-Schottky (MS) measurement (Fig. S17). The VB value of a-NiOx/CDs and a-NiOx are respectively 1.83 and 1.60 eV, and the conduction bands (CB) acquired from the MS curves are −0.80 and −0.86 V (vs Ag/AgCl), namely −0.58 and −0.64 V (vs NHE) (Fig. 6d). The bandgap expansion indicates the improved oxidation and reduction capacity of a-NiOx/CDs, and it could satisfy the conversion potential of the redox of Ni2+/Ni3+ and Eqs. (3) and (4).

Based on the above analysis, the possible catalytic mechanism of a-NiOx/CDs was deduced and depicted in Fig. 6e. Under visible light, because of the hole acceptance property [20,21], the embedded CDs effectually avoid the internal consumption of photocarriers caused by the intrinsic amorphous structure and prominently facilitate the shift of the photoinduced electrons. Afterwards, the cycling conversion between Ni2+ and Ni3+ are activated by the photoinduced electrons (Eq. (5)) and then triggers the chemical reactions on the active sites.

In the photocatalytic oxidation of TMB, CDs as the hole-rich sites benefits the generation of H2O2 and ∙O2 – (Equations 3–4). Besides, the conversion of Ni2+ to Ni3+ could also lead to the formation of ∙O2 – (Eq. (6)). As the main active radical in TMB photocatalysis, ∙O2 – oxidize TMB to the blue product TMB-ox (Eq. (7)). Meanwhile, h+ can also directly react with TMB to obtain their oxides (Eq. (8)).
(5)
Ni
3 +
+ e
-
→
Ni
2 +
(6)
Ni
2 +
+ O
2
→
Ni
3 +
+ ·
O
2
-
(7)
TMB +
·
O
2
-
= TMB - ox
(8)
TMB + h
+
= TMB - ox

As a multi-electron hydrogenation process, the p-NP reduction requires enough electrons and H*. As depicted in Fig. 6d, Ni2+ formed from Ni3+ through Eq. (5) would deliver its active electrons to the adsorbed p-NP and recover the original state. Besides, plenty of H* are continuously activated from NaBH4 on the nickel oxide since BH4 - anchors to the abundant active sites in nickel oxide by chemical adsorption. As a consequence, the sufficient supply of both electrons and H* contribute to the reduction reaction of p-NP (Eq. (9)), and the product p-AP then leave the Ni2+/Ni3+ defect to give room for another p-NP molecule. Therefore, the photoinduced electrons boost the p-NP reduction as well as the cyclic conversion between Ni2+ and Ni3+.
(9)
p - NP + H* + e
-
→
p - AP

In addition, the bacteriostatic experiment (Fig. 7 ) also confirms the higher density of ROS generated in a-NiOx/CDs than a-NiOx under illumination, since the high reactivity of ROS can destroy cell membranes, mRNA, DNA, ribosomes, peptidoglycan, and proteins [7]. Staphylococcus aureus was employed as human pathogenic bacteria that could parasitize on human body and cause severe metastatic infection when invade in lymphatic and blood. The antibacterial performance could be evaluated by the number of bacterial colonies on the culture medium after culturing for 24 h. In Fig. 8a, a-NiOx/CDs and a-NiOx both show sterilization effect without light due to the size-dependent toxicity of metallic nanoparticles that could cause modifications in the cell membrane and block the transport channels [7]. But the effect is limited that there are still many residual bacterial colonies. When visible light with 60 mW/cm2 is imposed on the liquid mixture for 10 min, there are no bacterial colonies exist in the a-NiOx/CDs system, while several were still scattered in the a-NiOx system. Therefore, the photogenerated ROS endow a-NiOx/CDs with excellent antibacterial property and opens up the application prospect in sewage bacteriostat or photodynamic therapy in vivo.

Overall, a new nickel-based amorphous photocatalyst a-NiOx/CDs was facilely prepared in the CDs colloidal suspension. The heterogeneous nucleation of nickel oxide on CDs reduced the size and thickness of the amorphous domain that could decrease the transfer distance of the photoinduced charges. Therefore, the embedded CDs overcome the internal photocarrier consumption and effectively improve the transfer efficiency of the photoinduced charges, resulting in the boosted photocatalytic activity in the redox reactions and antibacterial activity under visible light.

Tingting Cai: Data curation, Writing – original draft, Investigation. Qing Chang: Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis. Chaorui Xue: Investigation, Visualization. Ning Li: Formal analysis, Visualization. Jinlong Yang: Resources, Writing – review & editing. Shengliang Hu: Supervision, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank for financial support Fundamental Research Program of Shanxi Province of China (20210302123037), Research Project Supported by Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (2022-136), Specialized Research Fund for Sanjin Scholars Program of Shanxi Province of China, Key Research and Development projects of Lvliang City (2021GXYF-1-20), China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2022.09.117.

The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
